findings.title: Container runs as root (missing runAsNonRoot)
findings.description: Running a container as root in Kubernetes is a critical misconfiguration. If compromised, the attacker gains root access to the container, significantly increasing the risk of a container escape to the host system.
findings.fix:
Add to securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 1000

findings.title: Privileged container (privileged: true)
findings.description: A privileged container has access to all Linux capabilities and can directly access host devices. This is equivalent to running a process with root privileges directly on the host.

findings.title: Missing resource limits
findings.description: Without CPU and memory limits, a single Pod can consume all node resources and bring down other services — known as a resource exhaustion attack. The Kubernetes scheduler also cannot optimally place Pods without this information.
findings.fix:
Add to container spec:
  resources:
    limits:
      cpu: '500m'
      memory: '512Mi'
    requests:
      cpu: '250m'
      memory: '256Mi'

findings.title: Latest tag used for container image
findings.description: The 'latest' tag is not deterministic — each deployment may run a different image version. This makes auditing difficult, prevents reliable rollbacks, and can silently introduce vulnerabilities.
findings.fix: Use a specific tag, e.g. nginx:1.25.3 instead of nginx:latest

findings.title: allowPrivilegeEscalation not disabled
findings.description: Without 'allowPrivilegeEscalation: false', a process inside the container can gain more privileges than its parent process through setuid/setgid mechanisms, making privilege escalation attacks easier.

findings.title: Missing liveness and readiness probes
findings.description: Without health probes, Kubernetes cannot automatically detect and restart unhealthy containers. The application may be in a broken state while Kubernetes continues routing traffic to it.
findings.fix: Add livenessProbe and readinessProbe to the container spec.

findings.title: Root filesystem is not read-only
findings.description: Without readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, an attacker who compromises the container can write to the filesystem — installing tools, modifying configurations, or persisting malware across restarts.

findings.title: hostNetwork or hostPID enabled
findings.description: hostNetwork: true or hostPID: true removes network/process isolation between the container and the host. An attacker can see all network interfaces and processes running on the host system.
findings.fix: Remove 'hostNetwork: true' and 'hostPID: true' from the Pod spec.

findings.title: Secrets in plaintext environment variables
findings.description: Defining secrets directly as env var values means they are visible to anyone with kubectl describe access. Kubernetes Secret objects provide better access control and can be encrypted at rest.
findings.fix:
Use secretKeyRef instead of plain values:
  env:
    - name: DB_PASSWORD
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: myapp-secrets
          key: db-password
